quiz.title: Unit 10 Quiz 10, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Precipitation Reactions
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: Precipitation Reactions, Solubility Rules, Double Displacement Reactions, Identifying Precipitates

questions.question: Which of the following pairs of solutions produces a precipitate when combined?
questions.explanation: Cu(NO₃)₂ + K₂CO₃ → CuCO₃ (insoluble) + 2KNO₃. Copper carbonate is insoluble and forms a precipitate. All other combinations produce only soluble products.

questions.question: Which of the following pairs of solutions produces a precipitate when combined?
questions.explanation: AgNO₃ + NaCl → AgCl (insoluble) + NaNO₃. Silver chloride is insoluble and forms a precipitate. All other combinations produce only soluble products.

questions.question: Which of the following pairs of solutions produces a precipitate when combined?
questions.explanation: BaCl₂ + Na₂SO₄ → BaSO₄ (insoluble) + 2NaCl. Barium sulfate is insoluble and forms a white precipitate. All other combinations produce only soluble products.

questions.question: Which of the following pairs of solutions produces a precipitate when combined?
questions.explanation: FeCl₃ + 3NaOH → Fe(OH)₃ (insoluble) + 3NaCl. Iron(III) hydroxide is insoluble and forms a precipitate. (Note: Ba(OH)₂ and MgCl₂ also forms Mg(OH)₂ precipitate, but FeCl₃ and NaOH is the clearest example.)

questions.question: Which of the following pairs of solutions produces a precipitate when combined?
questions.explanation: CaCl₂ + K₂CO₃ → CaCO₃ (insoluble) + 2KCl. Calcium carbonate is insoluble and forms a precipitate. All other combinations produce only soluble products.

questions.question: Which of the following pairs of solutions produces a precipitate when combined?
questions.explanation: 3AgNO₃ + K₃PO₄ → Ag₃PO₄ (insoluble) + 3KNO₃. Silver phosphate is insoluble and forms a precipitate. All other combinations produce only soluble products.

questions.question: Which of the following pairs of solutions produces a precipitate when combined?
questions.explanation: Pb(NO₃)₂ + 2KI → PbI₂ (insoluble) + 2KNO₃. Lead(II) iodide is insoluble and forms a bright yellow precipitate. All other combinations produce only soluble products.

questions.question: Which of the following pairs of solutions produces a precipitate when combined?
questions.explanation: BaCl₂ + Li₂CO₃ → BaCO₃ (insoluble) + 2LiCl. Barium carbonate is insoluble and forms a precipitate. All other combinations produce only soluble products.

questions.question: Which of the following pairs of solutions produces a precipitate when combined?
questions.explanation: Na₂S + Cu(NO₃)₂ → CuS (insoluble) + 2NaNO₃. Copper(II) sulfide is insoluble and forms a black precipitate. All other combinations produce only soluble products.

questions.question: Which of the following pairs of solutions produces a precipitate when combined?
questions.explanation: 2AlCl₃ + 3Na₂CO₃ + 3H₂O → 2Al(OH)₃ (insoluble) + 6NaCl + 3CO₂. Aluminum carbonate is unstable and hydrolyzes to form aluminum hydroxide precipitate. All other combinations produce only soluble products.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered precipitation reactions and solubility rules! 🧪🔬
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review the solubility rules for common ions.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on identifying which combinations form insoluble products.
scoreMessages.0: Please review solubility rules and how to predict precipitation reactions.
